children.name: Platalea
children.description: Unmistakeable white or pink waterbirds with spatula-shaped bills swept through shallow water. The Eurasian Spoonbill has colonised the Netherlands and Belgium and is now annual in southern Sweden; breeding is expected.

children.name: Platalea leucorodia
children.description: A tall white waterbird that feeds by sweeping its spatulate bill through shallow water, snapping shut on small fish and invertebrates. After colonising the Low Countries it is now annual in southern Sweden, with breeding anticipated.

children.name: Platalea regia
children.description: A large white spoonbill of Australia, New Zealand, and Indonesia. In breeding plumage it develops a spectacular black spatulate crest and red patterning on the legs and bill.

children.name: Platalea alba
children.description: A pinkish-white spoonbill of African wetlands and lakes, distinguished from the Eurasian Spoonbill by its bright red legs and face skin. It feeds actively in shallow waters, sweeping its bill through the water in a wide arc.

children.name: Platalea flavipes
children.description: An Australian spoonbill with a distinctive yellow bill and legs, the only spoonbill with pale rather than dark legs. Unlike other spoonbills it feeds by sight more than by touch, wading through shallow wetlands and taking visible prey.

children.name: Platalea ajaja
children.description: A spectacular pink spoonbill of the Americas, ranging from the southern United States to Argentina. Its brilliant pink colour comes from carotenoid pigments in its diet of crustaceans. Breeds colonially in mangroves.

children.name: Platalea minor
children.description: One of the rarest spoonbills, breeding on small islands off the coasts of North Korea, South Korea, and China. It has a distinctive black face and bill. The global population is around 6000 individuals and is slowly recovering. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Plegadis
children.description: Dark, glossy waders with long decurved bills, expanding northward across Europe and the Americas. Their plumage glows with metallic iridescence in direct sunlight.

children.name: Plegadis falcinellus
children.description: A dark, glossy wader whose bronze-and-purple sheen catches the light as it probes wetland mud with a long decurved bill. Once a rarity, it is now a regular and increasing visitor to southern Sweden.

children.name: Plegadis chihi
children.description: The white-faced ibis is a wading bird in the ibis family, Threskiornithidae.

children.name: Plegadis ridgwayi
children.description: A dark ibis of the high Andean wetlands (puna) from Peru to northern Chile and Argentina. It has a greenish-glossed body and red legs, and is often seen in flocks probing the mud of shallow lakes at altitudes over 4000 m.

children.name: Threskiornis
children.description: The sacred ibises — white-plumaged ibises with naked black heads and necks, found across the Old World tropics. The African Sacred Ibis was revered in ancient Egypt as the embodiment of Thoth, the god of writing and wisdom, and was mummified in vast numbers at sites such as Saqqara.

children.name: Threskiornis aethiopicus
children.description: A white ibis with a black naked head and neck, revered in ancient Egypt as the living symbol of Thoth. It inhabits wetlands, grasslands, and urban areas across sub-Saharan Africa and was introduced to Europe, where it has become established in France and Italy.

children.name: Threskiornis melanocephalus
children.description: A large white ibis of South and Southeast Asian wetlands, with a naked black head and neck. It nests in colonies near water and has declined due to wetland drainage and disturbance. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Threskiornis moluccus
children.description: An adaptable ibis that has successfully colonised urban areas across Australia, where it is known colloquially as the 'bin chicken' for its habit of scavenging in rubbish bins. In natural wetlands it feeds on crayfish, frogs, and insects.

children.name: Threskiornis spinicollis
children.description: A distinctive Australian ibis with a black back and glossy rainbow-tinted wings, a white belly, and a collar of straw-like feather shafts on the neck. It is a highly nomadic species that follows rainfall to breed in temporary wetlands.

children.name: Threskiornis bernieri
children.description: A white ibis restricted to the coastal wetlands of western Madagascar and Aldabra Atoll. It has a bare black head and neck like its African relative but is smaller and has a reduced bill. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Threskiornis solitarius
children.description: A large, flightless ibis formerly endemic to the island of Réunion in the Indian Ocean. It had white plumage and a black naked head, and was hunted to extinction by European sailors in the early 18th century. It is the only species in the family known to have been entirely flightless.

children.name: Pseudibis
children.description: Two large, dark ibises of South and Southeast Asian lowlands, distinguished by their bare facial skin and white patches on the shoulders. They are among the most threatened ibises due to extensive habitat loss across their ranges.

children.name: Pseudibis papillosa
children.description: A dark brown ibis with a distinctive red patch on the nape and a long, slender decurved bill. It is common across the Indian subcontinent in wetlands, riversides, and irrigated farmland.

children.name: Pseudibis davisoni
children.description: A dark ibis with a distinctive white patch on the upper wing, found across Southeast Asia. It is critically endangered due to the loss of lowland forests and wetlands; fewer than 1000 individuals survive.

children.name: Pseudibis gigantea
children.description: The largest ibis in the world, standing up to 106 cm tall, with dark grey-brown plumage, a bare grey head, and a distinctive reddish cap. It is critically endangered and confined to remote wetlands and open forests in northern Cambodia, southern Laos, and Vietnam; fewer than 350 adults remain.

children.name: Geronticus
children.description: Two species of bald ibis with completely bare, featherless heads — in contrast to other ibises which have featherless areas only on the face. They nest on cliff ledges rather than in trees. The Northern Bald Ibis was once widespread across Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa; its disappearance from Europe is one of the best-documented avian extinctions on the continent.

children.name: Geronticus eremita
children.description: A bizarre, glossy black ibis with a red, featherless head and long curved bill. Once bred in central Europe (the Alps were a stronghold) and the Middle East; now critically endangered, with the only wild population in Morocco. A reintroduction programme is re-establishing a migratory population in Europe using ultralight aircraft to guide hand-raised birds.

children.name: Geronticus calvus
children.description: A dark ibis with a bare red crown and upper face, endemic to the highland grasslands of South Africa, Lesotho, and Eswatini. It breeds on cliff ledges and feeds on insects in montane grasslands. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Nipponia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Crested Ibis — one of the rarest birds in the world, brought back from the brink of extinction through intensive conservation. Its white plumage takes on a greyish-rose tint during the breeding season.

children.name: Nipponia nippon
children.description: A stunning white ibis with a red face, a shaggy crest of white feathers, and a long decurved bill. It was once widespread in East Asia but was thought extinct in the wild until seven birds were rediscovered in China in 1981. Captive breeding has grown the population to over 5000. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Bostrychia
children.description: Forest ibises of sub-Saharan Africa, mostly dark-plumaged inhabitants of swamp forests and woodlands. The Hadada Ibis is one of the most familiar birds of African gardens, named for its loud, raucous call that echoes across suburban Nairobi.

children.name: Bostrychia hagedash
children.description: A grey-brown ibis with a distinctive white moustache and iridescent wing patches. Its loud, laughing 'ha-da-da' call is one of the most characteristic sounds of sub-Saharan African mornings, often heard from gardens, golf courses, and wetlands.

children.name: Bostrychia carunculata
children.description: A dark ibis endemic to the highlands of Ethiopia, with a distinctive fleshy wattle at the base of the bill. It feeds in montane grasslands and breeds on rock ledges and cliffs.

children.name: Bostrychia olivacea
children.description: A dark olive-brown forest ibis of West and Central African lowland rainforests, with a greenish gloss on the wings. It is secretive and poorly known, often detected only by its loud call.

children.name: Bostrychia rara
children.description: A small, dark ibis of dense equatorial African forests, with distinctive white spots on the chest and neck. It skulks in swampy undergrowth and is rarely seen.

children.name: Bostrychia bocagei
children.description: A small, dark forest ibis endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, with a long, slender decurved bill and iridescent greenish gloss on the wings. It inhabits primary and secondary rainforest in the island's interior and is threatened by habitat loss; IUCN status: Critically Endangered.

children.name: Theristicus
children.description: Three or four species of boldly patterned South American ibises with pale bellies and dark wings, found in open grasslands, savannas, and wetlands from the lowlands to the high Andes.

children.name: Theristicus caerulescens
children.description: A slate-grey ibis of central South American wetlands, with a distinctive white forehead patch and a long, slender decurved bill. It is more aquatic than other Theristicus and is often seen wading in shallow water.

children.name: Theristicus caudatus
children.description: A large, striking ibis of open grasslands and savannas from Colombia to Argentina, with a buff-orange neck, grey back, and black belly. It is often seen in loose flocks probing for insects in recently burned fields.

children.name: Theristicus oculatus
children.description: A grey ibis of the Andes and Patagonia, with a distinctive dark face and pale eye. It is common in the high-altitude grasslands of Bolivia, Peru, Chile, and Argentina, probing for insects and worms in soft soil.

children.name: Theristicus branickii
children.description: A large ibis of the high Andes in Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, and northern Chile, very similar to the Black-faced Ibis but with a deeper buff neck and more extensive white in the wing. It inhabits puna grasslands and bogs at altitudes of 3,000–5,000 m, where it probes for invertebrates in soft soil.

children.name: Cercibis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Sharp-tailed Ibis, a large, long-legged ibis of South American tropical wetlands named for its exceptionally long, stiff tail feathers.

children.name: Cercibis oxycerca
children.description: A large, dark ibis of lowland swamps and wet savannas in Venezuela, Colombia, and the Guianas. It has a very long, graduated tail that projects well beyond the wingtips in flight, and a long, almost straight bill.

children.name: Mesembrinibis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Green Ibis, a stocky, dark forest ibis of Central and South America with a distinctive greenish gloss to its plumage.

children.name: Mesembrinibis cayennensis
children.description: A dark greenish-black ibis of lowland forest swamps and riversides from Costa Rica to Argentina. It is solitary or in pairs, feeding on insects, spiders, and small frogs in shallow water along forest streams.

children.name: Phimosus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Bare-faced Ibis, a small, dark ibis with a distinctive bare red face and bill, widespread across South American wetlands.

children.name: Phimosus infuscatus
children.description: A small, dark ibis with a bare red face and pinkish-red bill, common in marshes, rice fields, and wet pastures from Colombia to Argentina. It is highly social and forms large foraging flocks.

children.name: Eudocimus
children.description: Two brilliantly coloured American ibises — one white, one scarlet — both with the same elegant body shape and long, slender decurved bill. The Scarlet Ibis is the national bird of Trinidad and Tobago and one of the most vividly coloured birds in the world.

children.name: Eudocimus albus
children.description: The American white ibis is a species of bird in the ibis family, Threskiornithidae. It is found from the southern half of the US East Coast, along the Gulf Coast states and south through most of the Caribbean coastal regions of Central America. This particular ibis species is a medium-sized wading bird, possessing an overall white plumage with black wing-tips, and having the typical downward-curving bill of the ibises, though of a bright red-orange color, the same hue as its long legs.

children.name: Eudocimus ruber
children.description: A breathtakingly bright scarlet waterbird, the national bird of Trinidad and Tobago. Its colour comes from carotenoid pigments in its diet of crustaceans. It breeds colonially in mangrove swamps and coastal wetlands from Venezuela to Brazil.

children.name: Lophotibis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Madagascar Crested Ibis — the only ibis endemic to Madagascar and one of the most beautiful, with a spectacular iridescent crest and wing pattern.

children.name: Lophotibis cristata
children.description: A large, striking ibis of Madagascar's eastern rainforests and western dry forests, with a glossy green-and-purple crest, white wings with black tips, and a long decurved bill. It feeds on invertebrates and small vertebrates in the forest leaf litter.
